perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall use the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party solely for the Purpose and for no other purpose .
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to use the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party for the Purpose, but may consider other purposes it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'endeavor' makes the obligation to use the information solely for the stated purpose less strict. The phrase 'but may consider other purposes it deems appropriate' introduces a broad and subjective exception that could allow uses outside the originally intended scope.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. Using confidential information obtained under the pretense of a specific purpose for other, self-serving purposes could be construed as an unfair or deceptive act, especially if it harms the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that directly undermines the original obligation. The original text states the recipient 'shall' use the information solely for the purpose. The changed text replaces 'shall' with 'shall endeavor,' weakening the obligation, and adds 'but may consider other purposes it deems appropriate,' which directly contradicts the original exclusivity. This contradicts M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, as using confidential information for self-serving purposes outside the originally agreed scope could be considered an unfair or deceptive act, especially if it harms the Disclosing Party. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 state that unfair or deceptive practices are unlawful, which this change enables.

perturbation.original_text: In all cases, t he Recipient shall use reasonable measures that are at least as stringent as those used by the Recipient to protect its own information of a similar nature (and in no event shall the Re cipient use less than reasonable measures) to keep confidential and safeguard from theft loss and/or unpermitted uses the Confidential Information disclosed to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: In all cases, t he Recipient shall try to use measures to keep confidential and safeguard from theft loss and/or unpermitted uses the Confidential Information disclosed to Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall use reasonable measures that are at least as stringent as those used by the Recipient to protect its own information of a similar nature (and in no event shall the Re cipient use less than reasonable measures)' with 'shall try to use measures' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. 'Try' implies an effort but does not guarantee or mandate a specific level of security, creating ambiguity about the required protective actions. The minimum level of security is also removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93H requires businesses that own or license personal information of Massachusetts residents to maintain reasonable security procedures and practices to protect such information. The modified text's ambiguous obligation to 'try to use measures' doesn't ensure reasonable security procedures, potentially violating this law if personal information is involved.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the recipient 'shall use reasonable measures,' while the changed text softens this to 'shall try to use measures.' According to the Law Explanation, M.G.L. c. 93H requires businesses to maintain reasonable security procedures. The modified text's weaker obligation does not ensure reasonable security, potentially violating this law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Section 2 Regulations to safeguard personal information of commonwealth residents,' indicating a legal requirement for security measures. The change from 'shall use' to 'shall try' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, contradicting the intent of the law to ensure reasonable security.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures for Personal Data which shall be at least as protective of the confidentiality of such information as the safeguards for personal information set forth under applicable federal, state and local law .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall consider implementing security measures for Personal Data.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures' to 'shall consider implementing security measures' removes the mandatory obligation to implement and maintain security for personal data. This makes the protection of personal data discretionary rather than required.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93I, § 1 requires data collectors to provide notice of a security breach when there is a breach of security of a system. Without implementing security measures, the consideration of implementing doesn't require one to actually do it, thus is not sufficient to prevent a breach which means that they might not be adhering to this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93I, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93I DISPOSITIONS AND DESTRUCTION OF RECORDS Section 1 Definitions Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Definitions Section 1. As used in this chapter the following words shall, unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings:— ''Agency'', any county, city, town, or constitutional office or any agency thereof, including but not limited to, any department, division, bureau, board, commission or committee thereof, or any authority created by the general court to serve a public purpose, having either statewide or local jurisdiction. ''Data subject'', an individual to whom personal information refers. ''Person'', a natural person, corporation, association, partnership or other legal entity. ''Personal information'', a resident's first name and last name or first initial and last name in combination with any 1 or more of the following data elements that relate to the resident:— (a) Social Security number; (b) driver's license number or Massachusetts identification card number; (c) financial account number, or credit or debit card number, with or without any required security code, access code, personal identification number or password that would permit access to a resident's financial account; or (d) a biometric indicator. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the implementation and maintenance of security measures for personal data, aligning with the intent to protect such information under applicable laws. The changed text weakens this obligation to a mere 'consideration' of implementing security measures. The Law Explanation posits that this discretionary approach is insufficient to prevent a data breach, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93I, § 1, which requires notice of a security breach. While the provided law snippets do not explicitly detail the required security measures, they define 'personal information' and reference Chapter 93I, thus underscoring the legal context surrounding the protection of personal data. The change from 'shall implement' to 'shall consider' significantly reduces the legally required protection, creating a contradiction because the new text allows for non-compliance with laws designed to protect personal information.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall be disclosed i n written, electronic or other permanent form and shall be prominently identified as confidential using an appropriate legend, marking stamp, or other clear and conspicuous written identification which unambiguously indicates that the information being pro vided is the Disclosing Party 's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder may be identified as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the detailed requirements for identifying confidential information with 'may be identified as confidential' makes the marking of confidential information optional and removes any specific guidance on how to do so. This ambiguity allows for information to be disclosed without clear identification, making it difficult to enforce confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act, M.G.L. c. 93, § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act provides protection for trade secrets. For information to be considered a trade secret, reasonable efforts must be made to maintain its secrecy. The modified text's optional identification of confidential information does not ensure reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially undermining trade secret protection under this law, as it removes a key step in safeguarding this information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets under Massachusetts law. The original text mandates specific identification of confidential information, whereas the changed text makes it optional. The Law Explanation states that 'reasonable efforts must be made to maintain its secrecy' for information to be considered a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' related to trade secrets, implying a need for clear identification and protection to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure. Making the identification of confidential information optional undermines 'reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy', therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Recipient shall cease use of all Confidential Information furnished hereunder and shall, upon written direction of the Disclosing Party , return to the Disclosing Party , or destroy, all such Confidential Information, together with all copies made thereof by the Recipient . Upon request, the Recipient shall send the Disclosing Party a certificate con firming the destruction or return of all Confidential Information delivered hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Recipient should consider ceasing use of all Confidential Information furnished hereunder, and may return or destroy the information. Providing a certificate confirming destruction or return is at the Recipient's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall cease use' to 'should consider ceasing use' and 'shall return or destroy' to 'may return or destroy' makes the return or destruction of confidential information optional. Removing the mandatory nature of the certificate of destruction further weakens the obligation. This creates ambiguity about the ultimate fate of the confidential information after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. Refusing to return or destroy confidential information after the termination of the agreement, and failing to provide proof of such actions, could be considered an unfair practice if it creates a competitive disadvantage for the disclosing party or if the recipient then uses that information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The changed text undermines the legal obligation to protect confidential information, potentially creating an unfair practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. By changing 'shall cease use' to 'should consider ceasing use' and 'shall return or destroy' to 'may return or destroy,' the recipient is no longer obligated to protect the Disclosing Party's confidential information, nor required to prove they did so. As stated in the provided law snippets, Section 2(a) of Chapter 93A declares unlawful 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce.' The modification introduces ambiguity and makes the protection of confidential information discretionary, which could lead to unfair competitive practices.
